title: FIFA World Cup 2026 to Challenge Mexico’s Digital Infrastructure - Mexico Business News
snippet: FIFA World Cup 2026 to Challenge Mexico’s Digital Infrastructure  Mexico Business News
content:
FIFA World Cup 2026 to Challenge Mexico’s Digital Infrastructure
Less than one year before the FIFA World Cup 2026 begins, Mexico faces the challenge of upgrading its digital infrastructure to meet the stringent technological standards set by FIFA. The country shows a measurable lag in connectivity and spectrum availability compared to its co-hosts, the United States and Canada, requiring accelerated execution of public-private strategies to ensure the event's operational viability.
The push for this technological modernization stems not from a domestic government initiative, but from a direct requirement imposed by FIFA. According to Norberto Maldonado, Managing Partner, Sellcom Solutions, the technology upgrades are a FIFA requirement, not a request by the Mexican government.
The 2026 FIFA World Cup will be unprecedented in scale and complexity. It will be the first edition with 48 teams and 104 matches in three host countries, which significantly increases the demand for technological resources compared to previous tournaments. FIFA's requirements aim to standardize a high-quality experience for media, teams, and global audiences.
Previous tournaments established a clear precedent. In Russia 2018, the focus was on robust 4G/LTE networks and the introduction of video assistant referee (VAR) systems with 4K broadcasts. In Qatar 2022, the leap was the widespread deployment of 5G networks in stadiums and high-density areas by operators such as Ericsson and Ooredoo. This enabled gigabit speeds and managed unprecedented data traffic spikes. During the semifinal between France and Morocco, data exchanges exceeded 132TB in a single minute, a milestone demonstrating the critical importance of telecommunications infrastructure.
For 2026, this level of demand is expected not only to be matched but significantly surpassed. Mexico, which will host the opening match at Azteca Stadium, now Banorte Stadium, and additional matches in Guadalajara and Monterrey, must ensure its infrastructure can support this traffic volume and offer advanced digital services.
Current Infrastructure Assessment
Mexico's main obstacle is the telecommunications infrastructure gap compared to its North American partners. Data from Ookla shows that Mexico's average fixed broadband download speed is 90Mbps, far below the United States with 291Mbps and Canada with 243Mbps. The disparity is more pronounced in mobile broadband, where Mexico averages 48Mbps, compared to 176Mbps in the United States and 105Mbps in Canada.
Compounding this issue is a historical delay in radio spectrum allocation, essential for 5G deployment. Temporary licenses have been granted, such as an experimental concession to Televisión Azteca in the 600MHz band for 8K test transmissions. However, these are stopgap measures and do not address the structural deficit.
Various entities have launched initiatives to close the gap, reports Proceso. In January 2025, the Federal Telecommunications Institute (IFT) approved the Federal Electricity Commission’s (CFE) acquisition of a stake in Altán Redes to reactivate the Red Compartida, a wholesale 4G infrastructure network. The IFT also established a 5G technical committee to coordinate and assess the rollout of this technology.
Efforts are also underway to expand high-capacity fiber optic links through alliances such as CFE-Telmex. Expansion of internet node networks and data centers is also in progress. In the private sector, sponsors such as Visa are promoting digital payments to make stadiums cashless environments, equipping venues, public transportation, and nearby businesses with terminals and mobile app support.
Finally, emerging technologies are expected to enhance the event experience. Maldonado highlights the introduction of an AI-assisted VAR system, where an algorithm will analyze plays to reduce human error. Stadiums in Mexico, particularly the Azteca Stadium, are expected to offer immersive experiences using virtual reality devices, allowing spectators to view matches from premium perspectives in real time. Security will also incorporate AI, with facial and behavioral monitoring systems for access control and risk detection.
These infrastructure investments could be turned into permanent assets that accelerate Mexico’s digital transformation, says Maldonado to MVS Noticias. Otherwise, there is a risk of generating costly and underutilized infrastructure. He adds that the massive data processing required by the event will take place in local data centers, creating a significant carbon footprint that must be addressed in the tournament's sustainability planning.
Fixed and mobile data consumption during the 2026 World Cup is expected to break all previous records. With global internet penetration now above 70% (compared to 62% in 2022) and a growing number of connected devices, each match will generate hundreds of thousands of simultaneous connections.

title: NY/NJ Reveal 2026 World Cup Brand and Infrastructure - amNewYork
snippet: NY/NJ Reveal 2026 World Cup Brand and Infrastructure  amNewYork
content:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup New York/New Jersey (NYNJ) host committee rang the New York Stock Exchange Opening Bell Monday morning to commemorate a projected $3.3 billion boon from the tournament, according to a new Economic Impact Summary released today by the host committee and Tourism Economics, a journal owned by Oxford Economics.
According to the summary, more than 1.2 million visitors are expected to travel to the NY/NJ region during the tournament, including eight matches hosted at MetLife Stadium, where a Round of 32, Round of 16, and the Final will be played. A whopping $431.9 million will be generated from state and local tax revenues, while the projected spending figure is at $1.7 billion.
“The Economic Impact Summary underscores the region’s unique position as a global destination capable of hosting the world’s largest single-sport event,” Alex Lasry, CEO of the NYNJ Host Committee, said. “With world-class infrastructure, cultural diversity, and unmatched connectivity, NYNJ is poised to deliver a memorable FIFA World Cup experience—on and off the pitch.”
The World Cup Final is set to take place at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, NJ — home of the New York Giants and Jets — on July 19, 2026, following a month-long festival of football up and down the North American continent. The tournament starts at the Estadio Azteca in Mexico City on July 11, where Mexico will kick off the tournament in a stadium that has hosted two World Cup Finals previously.
“Long before that first [pass], we know that the work is already well underway and our momentum is real,” NY/NJ host committee chair, Tammy Murphy, said at the bell-ringing ceremony. “The FIFA Club World Cup Final, along with the international fanfare it generated, was just a preview of the world-class experience we plan to deliver next summer, but it was also an opportunity to show that our region is ready, our venues are world-class, and our people are eager to welcome the world.”
The Club World Cup Final on July 13, where Chelsea brushed Paris St. Germain aside in a 3-0 win, saw an almost-packed out Metlife Stadium for the first time in the tournament. The match was attended by 81,118 people, and also saw President Donald Trump join the Chelsea squad for their trophy lift.
In addition to the games, the host committee announced on June 13 that Liberty State Park will host the official FIFA Fan Festival, where the park “will create a high-energy destination for fans and families from across the globe to watch the matches with an open and accessible environment,” Murphy said. “It will be a celebration that is uniquely reflective of the culture, the creativity, and the diversity of New York and New Jersey.”
Murphy also revealed plans for other fan zones across the five boroughs and New Jersey, while highlighting their FIFA Skyline to Shoreline Road Tour, a mobile fan zone stuffed with games, merchandise, and music. They have already made stops at a pair of New York Red Bulls games, including the 5-1 loss to Inter Miami this past weekend, and future stops can be found here.
”It is a regional celebration of soccer, music, food and culture,” Murphy said. “It will generate jobs, boost tourism, and create an immersive and exciting environment for fans to enjoy the games, and will be a critical piece of our commitment to ensuring that this World Cup is not just something we watch. It’s something we feel, whether or not you have tickets. That’s what it’s all about, building on the excitement, bringing it into our neighborhoods, and making sure every single person feels a part of this historic moment, and as we build toward that moment, we are already on the move.”
For more on the 2026 FIFA World Cup, visit AMNY.com

title: Houston begins 100-day countdown to 2026 FIFA World Cup with infrastructure updates, events and displays - Community Impact | News
snippet: Houston begins 100-day countdown to 2026 FIFA World Cup with infrastructure updates, events and displays  Community Impact | News
content:
What's new
The city has begun to celebrate with visual displays, including the illumination of prominent buildings in Downtown Houston's skyline. City Hall, 1000 Main and the Bank of America Tower will now shine blue, Houston's official FIFA World Cup color, to celebrate the milestone.
Officials with the display said they encourage residents to join the celebration by posting their photos of the buildings on social media, using #FIFAWorldCup.In similar news
Aside from visual displays, some entities within Houston are also beginning to prep infrastructure for the arrival of the tournament and the influx of visitors it will bring in June.
CenterPoint Energy officials said in a March 4 news release that the company's electric, natural gas and emergency response teams have started to prepare as tens of thousands of "enthusiastic fans gear up to descend on Houston."
What's happening?
CenterPoint is coordinating with local stakeholders and leaders to perform hazard mitigation inspections of the company's equipment and field reviews serving event-zone areas, such as NRG Stadium, airports and key hospitality locations.
“The World Cup is a once-in-a-generation opportunity for Houston", CenterPoint Chief Operating Officer Jesus Soto said in the release. "Our teams are committed to powering every moment, whether it’s the lights at NRG Stadium, hotels hosting international guests, or the fan festivals downtown. The preparations we’ve been taking are crucial to making this event a success.”CenterPoint's natural gas operations teams are also carrying out a comprehensive readiness plan to support World Cup activities, per the release. The plan includes performing inspections and making any needed repairs to critical sites across the city. Daily patrols in specific areas have also been implemented, with coverage expected to expand before matches begin in the summer.
Also of note
Houston City Council also applied for an $80 million grant in February to enhance security at the 2026 FIFA World Cup and begin preparation for the public safety aspect of the tournament.
The grant is expected to help enhance public safety initiatives and allow the purchase of safety equipment, traffic and crowd management, communication tools and drones, according to previous Community Impact coverage.
Looking ahead
As the countdown to the tournament continues, several events and milestones are expected to take place across the city over the next few months, including:
- Road to the Cup: A 7v7 youth soccer tournament for boys and girls ages U11 through U18/19 will take place March 6-8.
- 50-day countdown: April 2 will mark 50 days until the start of the tournament.
- Fan Festival: Construction of the FIFA Fan Festival in East Downtown will start May 1.
- Trophy Tour by Coca-Cola: Fans will be able to see the original FIFA World Cup Trophy as it tours across North America. The tour will hit Houston on May 2-3.

content:

title: California Joins Texas, New York, Florida, Massachusetts, New Jersey, and Others in Supercharging US Tourism for 2026 FIFA World Cup with Major Infrastructure, Stadium Upgrades, and More: Everything You Need To Know - Travel And Tour World
snippet: California Joins Texas, New York, Florida, Massachusetts, New Jersey, and Others in Supercharging US Tourism for 2026 FIFA World Cup with Major Infrastructure, Stadium Upgrades, and More: Everything You Need To Know  Travel And Tour World
content:
Published on November 22, 2025
As the 2026 FIFA World Cup approaches, California joins Texas, New York, Florida, Massachusetts, New Jersey, and others in supercharging U.S. tourism with major infrastructure and stadium upgrades to host the global event. These states are investing heavily in enhancements that will not only accommodate the thousands of international visitors expected, but also leave a lasting impact on local economies and communities. From improved transit systems to transformed stadiums, each region is preparing to provide a seamless and unforgettable experience for fans. Here’s everything you need to know about the preparations shaping up across the country.
New York is undergoing major infrastructure upgrades to prepare for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. One of the most significant developments is the $2 billion AirTrain Newark project, which aims to improve transit connections between Newark Liberty International Airport and the surrounding areas. This new system will offer faster and more reliable transport options for the thousands of international visitors expected for the event. Additionally, MetLife Stadium in New Jersey, which will host the World Cup Final, is making modifications to accommodate the wider natural grass pitch required by FIFA. These changes, combined with the region’s ongoing investments, are expected to leave a lasting legacy of improved transportation and stadium facilities.
In New Jersey, MetLife Stadium is undergoing significant changes to prepare for the 2026 World Cup, where it will host the prestigious World Cup Final. The stadium will have to make adjustments to its lower bowl seating to allow space for a wider natural grass pitch, as mandated by FIFA. Beyond the stadium itself, the region is focused on capitalizing on the event to boost its economy, with projections estimating a $3.3 billion economic impact and the creation of over 26,000 jobs. Additionally, Liberty State Park in New Jersey will host the official FIFA Fan Festival, further cementing the region’s role as a key destination during the tournament.
California is undergoing extensive renovations to its stadiums in preparation for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. Both SoFi Stadium in Los Angeles and Levi’s Stadium in the San Francisco Bay Area are making necessary adjustments to accommodate FIFA’s requirements, including replacing or retracting lower-bowl seating to create space for the wider natural grass pitch. SoFi Stadium will play a major role in hosting the US Men’s National Team opener and a Quarter-final match. Alongside these stadium changes, California is using the World Cup to accelerate public transit improvements in Los Angeles, while the San Francisco Bay Area is focusing on enhancing emergency response readiness through large-scale disaster drills.
Advertisement
Advertisement
Texas is investing heavily in infrastructure improvements as Dallas and Houston prepare for the 2026 World Cup. Dallas will serve as the tournament’s operational hub, with the Kay Bailey Hutchison Convention Center transforming into the International Broadcast Center (IBC) and Tournament Operations Center. To support this, Dallas is investing up to $15 million in capital improvements, including roof repairs, drainage upgrades, and climate control. In Houston, the city is revitalizing its downtown with the creation of the Main Street Promenade, a new pedestrian district designed to improve walkability and green spaces. Both cities will also replace artificial turf with temporary natural grass fields in their stadiums to comply with FIFA’s requirements.
Atlanta is focusing on sustainability and technology as it prepares to host a Semi-Final match at Mercedes-Benz Stadium during the 2026 World Cup. The stadium is undergoing modifications to replace its artificial turf with a natural grass field, which will be installed within its retractable roof. In addition to stadium upgrades, the city is focusing on sustainability initiatives, such as reducing environmental impacts and making its facilities more energy-efficient. The Centennial Yards project, located near the stadium, is a major development aimed at creating a lasting legacy of improved infrastructure. With its proximity to Atlanta’s airport and downtown, the city is well-positioned to serve as a model for future international events.
Advertisement
Advertisement
Miami is preparing for the 2026 World Cup with a strong focus on hospitality and transportation upgrades. Hard Rock Stadium, which will host seven matches, including the Third Place Play-off and a Quarter-final, is undergoing minor renovations to install a temporary natural grass pitch. The city is also working closely with regional tourism partners to manage the influx of international visitors. This includes enhancing transportation services and improving hospitality infrastructure to ensure a smooth experience for visitors. Miami’s status as a global tourist destination will further be reinforced during the tournament, showcasing its world-class appeal to football fans from across the globe.
Boston is preparing for the 2026 World Cup by making significant renovations to Gillette Stadium, which will host several matches during the tournament. The stadium is undergoing multi-phase upgrades to enhance premium seating areas and improve fan amenities. Additionally, the venue’s artificial turf will be replaced with temporary natural grass to meet FIFA’s specifications. To accommodate the expected large crowds, Boston’s transportation authorities are developing comprehensive plans to manage crowds and improve access to the Foxborough stadium, which is located outside the city center. These changes will showcase the renovated stadium and contribute to Boston’s growing reputation as a top-tier sports destination.
Advertisement
Advertisement
Beyond California, states such as Georgia and Massachusetts are likewise ramping up for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. In Georgia, Atlanta’s transit authority is deploying new electric buses and railcars, and Mercedes-Benz Stadium is earmarked for roughly $200 million in upgrades ahead of its semi-final match. In Massachusetts, the region surrounding Gillette Stadium is undergoing infrastructure improvements, including the expansion of the Foxboro commuter rail station to handle thousands of fans per match. These efforts illustrate how multiple states are leveraging the tournament to drive long-term infrastructure and tourism gains.
California is joining key states like Texas, New York, Florida, Massachusetts, and New Jersey in supercharging U.S. tourism for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. Through major infrastructure improvements, stadium upgrades, and strategic investments, these states are enhancing their appeal as global destinations. With the tournament expected to draw millions of international visitors, these efforts will leave a lasting impact on transportation, hospitality, and local economies, making the U.S. a prime host for this prestigious event. The collective preparations promise a world-class experience for fans and a booming tourism industry.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026: Houston to receive nearly $10M to enhance public transportation - FOX 26 Houston
snippet: FIFA World Cup 2026: Houston to receive nearly $10M to enhance public transportation  FOX 26 Houston
content:
FIFA World Cup 2026: Houston to receive nearly $10M to enhance public transportation
HOUSTON - Almost $10 million in grant money is coming to Houston ahead of the World Cup.
Houston to receive almost $10M to enhance public transportation ahead of 2026 World Cup
What we know:
U.S. Senator John Cornyn announced on Thursday that the U.S. Department of Transportation said public transit systems across Houston were awarded almost $10 million dollars ahead of the FIFA World Cup.
According to the release, the funding is to enhance public transportation and ensure the safe travel of fans and athletes ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
What they're saying:
"The 2026 World Cup is just around the corner, which will bring hundreds of thousands of fans to Houston in need of a safe mode of transportation to seamlessly travel to and from stadiums," said Sen. Cornyn. "I’m grateful these resources will enhance public transit to help meet the expected surge in demand and create memorable experiences for players, teams, and fans. I’m proud to also have secured a historic investment of $625 million in President Trump’s Working Families Tax Cuts Act to strengthen public safety for FIFA host cities, including Dallas and Houston."
The backstory:
According to the statement, the federal transportation grant funding was made possible through the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2026.
Officials said Cornyn hosted a roundtable in Dallas earlier in the year to highlight the law enforcement partnerships, security planning, and public safety apparatus in place to prepare for the games and protect fans.
The World Cup is expected to have a more than $1.5 billion economic impact and attract hundreds of thousands of visitors to Houston, which is set to host seven FIFA matches at NRG Stadium, officials said.
Dig deeper:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup transportation grant funding will, according to officials:
- Support planning, capital, and operating expenses for matches and other public events held in the host city;
- Account for stadium capacity and number of matches;
- And require transit agencies to obligate these funds within one fiscal year of the close of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
The Source: Statement from Sen. John Cornyn's Office

title: DOT Announces $100M Funding to Enhance Public Transportation in 2026 FIFA World Cup Host Cities - Homeland Security Today
snippet: DOT Announces $100M Funding to Enhance Public Transportation in 2026 FIFA World Cup Host Cities  Homeland Security Today
content:
U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean P. Duffy has announced the Federal Transit Administration (FTA) is investing $100.3 million into public transit systems within host cities preparing to welcome millions of fans during the 2026 FIFA World Cup. The funding will ensure communities can expand transit options to meet increased demand in services around stadiums.
“DOT is doing its part to ensure the United States is ready to host the most spectacular World Cup in history,” said U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean P. Duffy. “We’re proud to partner with host cities to provide fans with a seamless travel experience that will be part of the memories they cherish from attending these historic games.”
“Thanks to President Trump’s vision, we are entering a new American era of sports and global events,” said Executive Director of the White House Task Force on the FIFA World Cup 2026 Andrew Giuliani. “This funding is about more than moving fans—it’s about preparing our communities to host the largest sporting event in history and ensuring the world sees America at its best. These investments will help create lasting memories for visitors and residents alike, and reinforce our commitment to safety, hospitality, and operational excellence.”
The 2026 FIFA World Cup Funding:
- Will support planning, capital, and operating expenses supporting matches and other public events held in U.S. host cities.
- Will be apportioned by a formula accounting for stadium capacity and number of matches in each host city.
- Allows for 100 percent Federal share for these funds.
- Requires transit agencies to obligate these funds within one fiscal year of the close of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

title: Seattle Black Limo Releases Transportation Advisory Ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026 Match Days at Lumen Field - USA Today
snippet: Seattle Black Limo Releases Transportation Advisory Ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026 Match Days at Lumen Field  USA Today
content:
February 28, 2026 — Seattle Black Limo, a private chauffeur transportation company operating in Seattle since 2010, has released a transportation advisory related to FIFA World Cup 2026 travel planning. According to the company, visitors, football fans, and corporate travelers may benefit from arranging transportation in advance as match-day traffic demand increases. Travelers can review transportation options through the company’s booking platform at https://www.seattleblacklimo.com/.
Seattle Selected as FIFA World Cup 2026 Host City
Seattle is one of 16 host cities selected across North America for the FIFA World Cup 2026. Six matches are scheduled at Lumen Field between June 15 and July 6. The tournament is expected to bring a high volume of international visitors through Seattle-Tacoma International Airport during the event period. According to publicly available information, increased traffic congestion and parking limitations are expected near Lumen Field on match days.
Transportation Demand Expected to Increase
According to Seattle Black Limo, corporate groups and travel delegations have begun arranging transportation for the tournament period. The company stated that higher demand for rideshare services and increased traffic restrictions around Lumen Field may affect transportation availability and travel times during match days. Travelers planning to attend events may consider arranging transportation ahead of time to support smoother travel planning.
Fleet Preparation for Tournament Period
Seattle Black Limo stated that its chauffeurs monitor flight schedules and traffic conditions to support transportation coordination during busy travel periods. The company’s fleet includes executive sedans, SUVs, and Mercedes Executive Sprinter vans intended to accommodate both individual travelers and groups.
Regional Transportation Coverage
According to the company, transportation services are available across the greater Seattle area, including Bellevue, Redmond, Kirkland, Tacoma, and Everett. Airport meet-and-greet services are also available for travelers arriving at Seattle-Tacoma International Airport.
About Seattle Black Limo
Seattle Black Limo is a licensed and BBB-accredited chauffeur transportation company headquartered at 2640 S Holly St, Seattle, WA 98108. The company operates 24 hours a day, seven days a week, providing transportation services including airport transfers, wedding transportation, cruise port transfers at Pier 66 and Pier 91, and long-distance rides to Vancouver, BC and Portland, OR.

title: State MARTA committee discusses transportation plans for FIFA World Cup 2026 - Atlanta News First
snippet: State MARTA committee discusses transportation plans for FIFA World Cup 2026  Atlanta News First
content:
State MARTA committee discusses transportation plans for FIFA World Cup 2026
ATLANTA, Ga. (Atlanta News First) — A state committee in charge of overseeing MARTA held a meeting on Thursday morning to discuss plans for the FIFA World Cup 2026.
Atlanta’s Mercedes-Benz Stadium will host eight games, including a semifinal. City officials estimate the city will see more than 300,000 visitors during the event, which runs from June 11 to July 19.
PREVIOUS COVERAGE: Here are which teams will play in Atlanta during the 2026 World Cup
During the meeting, Jonathan Hunt, MARTA’s interim General Manager and CEO, shared how the transportation agency is preparing to handle large crowds.
“We are very, very excited for the World Cup,” he said.
Hunt said they have a comprehensive plan that includes operational safety, communication enhancements and cybersecurity.
MARTA will offer extended services, as well as increase the number of officers, staff, safety teams and transit ambassadors.
“We’re focused on cleanliness, lighting, additional staffing, and overall appearance of the system,” he said.
Ahead of the World Cup, MARTA will roll out several new services and transit system improvements.
In April, it’ll launch Better Breeze — an easier, faster way to pay.
“You can tap with a credit card. You can get a Breeze card. You can use your smart device if you want. You can use your watch to pay,” Hunt said.
And in the first week of June, MARTA will introduce replacement trains to its existing fleet.
Hunt said the work they’re doing now will not only prepare MARTA for the games, but also benefit the region for years to come.
“Certainly the World Cup was a catalyst, but the vast majority of the money that we’re spending now is going to benefit the region well beyond the World Cup,” Hunt said.
Hunt said they’ll also ensure people can find their way around using multiple languages.

title: Safety, transportation Dallas officials’ top priorities ahead of 2026 FIFA World Cup - KERA News
snippet: Safety, transportation Dallas officials’ top priorities ahead of 2026 FIFA World Cup  KERA News
content:
Safety, transportation Dallas officials’ top priorities ahead of 2026 FIFA World Cup
Dallas city officials are looking at how to handle all that comes with being a 2026 FIFA World Cup host city. Elected officials wanted to know more about safety plans and what the potential economic impact could be during Wednesday’s briefing.
The tournament starts in June 2026 — a little more than a year away. AT&T Stadium in Arlington will play host to nine FIFA matches, while Dallas’ Fair Park — and other venues across the city — will be used for the tournament’s fan festivals.
The Dallas City Council voted to approve a contract to make the Kay Bailey Hutchison Convention Center the tournament’s International Broadcast Center. The venue will host thousands of journalists from all over the world coming to Dallas to cover the World Cup.
“The World Cup will be the largest event Dallas has ever hosted,” Dallas City Manager Kimberly Bizor Tolbert said during Wednesday’s council meeting. “As you can imagine hosting an event of this scale requires meticulous planning at every single level.”
But a global event comes with a lot of security needs and council members wanted more information.
“Is Dallas Police Department going to need more help?” District 6 Council Member Omar Narvaez asked during the meeting. “Are there other law enforcement agencies that we would be bringing in to do certain things?”
Police officials said they were looking at staffing levels, additional security hired outside of the department and mutual aid.
District 12 Council Member Cara Mendelsohn, who chairs the Public Safety Committee, said there wasn’t much more elected officials could add to the security aspect — other than “being able to help secure additional dollars” for the department.
But she did have one question.
“[Will] there be any kind of change in the city’s posture in addressing homelessness?” Mendelsohn asked during the meeting.
Tolbert said the work the city is currently doing will continue moving forward.
“We are working very closely with the [Dallas] Sports Commission but the team that’s actually on the ground,” Tolbert said. “This is a collaborative process that’s going to definitely involve all of the key partners across the region…homelessness will definitely be one of those issues we continue to address.”
Mayor Pro Tem Tennell Atkins stressed the importance of transportation during the event. Although the matches will be held in Arlington — and there is no light rail connection to the city — Atkins said he wanted to make sure City Hall is supporting Dallas Area Rapid Transit (DART).
“Without transportation [the World Cup] would fail…because people cannot get where they need to go on a timeline,” Atkins said.
Some council members had questions about contingency plans if there is a natural disaster. Narvaez wanted to know what happens if, say, there’s a hurricane in Houston.
“…Because usually we’re the ones that help out in that situation,” Narvaez said.
Officials said FIFA hadn’t released its plans to the city but it also hadn’t asked Dallas to take on more games in case a natural disaster does happen.
Deputy Mayor Pro Tem Adam Bazaldua was more interested in what happens to potential evacuees in that situation and wanted to know if the city was prepared for that scenario while hosting the World Cup.
“Internally the city’s got a public safety working group…I know they are looking at all of that,” Rocky Vaz, the tournament’s Director of City Services and the International Broadcast Center, said.
Convention center officials said the tournament could bring in billions in economic impact. The 2026 World Cup begins June 11 with two matches — one in Guadalajara, Mexico and the other in Mexico City.

title: Kansas City takes steps to ensure transportation for FIFA World Cup 2026 - KCTV
snippet: Kansas City takes steps to ensure transportation for FIFA World Cup 2026  KCTV
content:
Kansas City takes steps to ensure transportation for FIFA World Cup 2026
KANSAS CITY, Mo. (KCTV) - There is a new person in charge of making sure Kansas City has adequate transportation when FIFA World Cup 2026 visits the area.
KC2026, the nonprofit organization in charge of planning, and executing, the massive event named Julie Lorenz as Senior Advisor for Transportation.
The organization said Lorenz will work with the transportation team to identify and implement transportation options that will benefit the Kansas City metro for decades to come.
Transportation is a key issue for FIFA World Cup 26. Teams will be spread throughout the region using different sites as practice locations and base camps. As part of the pitch to become a host city, Kansas City leaders ensured FIFA that there would be adequate transportation to handle teams, as well as the thousands of fans who are expected to visit the area.
ALSO READ: Missing gator from petting zoo was found safe after nearly two weeks
“As a longtime member of the Kansas City region, I’m proud to join the team in charge of executing the largest sporting event in this region’s history,” Lorenz said. “The transportation needs of this project are considerable, and I look forward to leveraging my experience in both the public and private sectors to deliver robust transportation plans.”
Kansas City will host six games, including four group-stage matches, one round of 32 and one quarterfinal game. The matches will be played at GEHA Field at Arrowhead Stadium. Construction for the event is already underway at Arrowhead.
The group-stage games are scheduled for:
- Tuesday, June 16th
- Saturday, June 20th
- Thursday, June 25th
- Saturday, June 27th
The round of 32 is scheduled for Friday, July 3. Kansas City will also host a quarter-final matchup on Saturday, July 11.

Other FIFA World Cup 2026 host cities include Atlanta, Boston, Dallas, Guadalajara, Houston, Kansas City, Los Angeles, Mexico City, Miami, Monterrey, New York/New Jersey, Philadelphia, San Francisco, Seattle, Toronto and Vancouver.
The 2026 World Cup will be the first hosted in the US since 1994.

Luxury car service prepares for global tourism surge with airport transfers and match-day transportation to MetLife Stadium
Meet Thaer - one of our most charming chauffeurs, driving with Legends for over 15 years 💎He recalls a moment when a client mistakenly booked a ride to the wrong airport. Instead of panic, Thaer offered calm reassurance, quickly rerouted, and still delivered her to the correct airport in time.This is what Legends does: not just transportation, but peace of mind and memorable experiences ✨Ready to ride legendary? New York, NY, March 19, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- New York, NY – With the 2026 FIFA World Cup expected to bring millions of international visitors to North America, transportation providers in the New York metropolitan region are preparing for one of the largest travel surges in the area’s history.
Several tournament matches will take place at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey—just minutes from Manhattan—making the New York–New Jersey region one of the primary gateways for fans, teams, sponsors, and international media traveling to the event.
In anticipation of the influx, Legends Limousine Worldwide, a New York–based luxury chauffeur transportation provider, announced expanded services designed to accommodate World Cup travelers arriving through John F. Kennedy International Airport (JFK), LaGuardia Airport (LGA), Newark Liberty International Airport (EWR), and Teterboro Airport.
A Legends chauffeur provides the first-class, reliable service the company is famous for.
“The World Cup will place extraordinary pressure on local transportation infrastructure,” said Amanda, Director of Sales for Legends Limousine Worldwide. “Visitors coming from around the world will need reliable transportation between airports, hotels, fan events, and the stadium. Our goal is to ensure travelers can move around the region safely, comfortably, and on schedule during what is set to be the biggest sporting event ever recorded.”
The company’s fleet—including luxury sedans, black SUVs, Sprinter vans, stretch limousines, and executive buses—will support airport pickups, hotel transfers, and group transportation for fans attending matches at MetLife Stadium.
Game days are expected to create heavy traffic across Manhattan, the Lincoln Tunnel corridor, and northern New Jersey, making advance transportation planning critical for visitors unfamiliar with the region’s transportation system.
Legends Limousine also plans to support corporate hospitality programs, media transportation, and VIP travel surrounding the tournament, as global sponsors and organizations host events throughout New York City.
With international tourism projected to reach record levels during the competition, travelers planning to attend matches in the New York area are encouraged to arrange airport transfers and stadium transportation well in advance.
Additional information and reservations are available at legendslimousine.com.

title: Houston to receive $9M grant to enhance public transportation for 2026 FIFA World Cup - Community Impact | News
snippet: Houston to receive $9M grant to enhance public transportation for 2026 FIFA World Cup  Community Impact | News
content:
After it was announced in late February that city officials would apply for $80 million in federal grants to enhance security at the tournament, Houston is now set to receive a new grant for transportation aid.
The gist
The U.S. Department of Transportation announced in early March that the public transit system across Houston will be awarded a $9 million grant through the Federal Transit Administration as part of a larger $100 million investment to improve public transportation for cities hosting World Cup matches.
Sen. John Cornyn, R-Houston, said in a March 5 news release that the grant will help ensure "safe travel of fans and athletes" ahead of the tournament by:
- Supporting planning, capital and operating expenses for matches and other public events held in the host city
- Accounting for stadium capacity and number of matches
- Requiring transit agencies to obligate these funds within one fiscal year of the close of the 2026 FIFA World Cup
One more thing
The funding for the grant will come through the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2026, which was signed into law in early February and provides appropriations to several federal departments for the remainder of fiscal year 2025-26, according to the Congress website.

title: NJ Transit to boost public transportation ahead of 2026 World Cup - Yahoo

content:
NJ Transit to boost public transportation ahead of 2026 World Cup
NEW JERSEY (PIX11) – New Jersey officials are gearing up for major transportation demand during the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
NJ Transit will provide expanded bus service to and from MetLife Stadium during the 2026 FIFA World Cup under a proposed agreement with the New Jersey Turnpike Authority.
FIFA will hold eight tournament matches at MetLife Stadium, including the World Cup final on July 19, 2026. The stadium sits along the New Jersey Turnpike, accessible from Exit 16W to the north and Exit 18W to the south.
Turnpike Authority staff recommended partnering with NJ Transit to encourage public transit use and reduce congestion. The Authority noted that heavy traffic increases crash risks, driver frustration, speeding and road‑rage incidents.
More: Latest News from Around the Tri-State
Under the plan, NJ Transit will add 85 buses staged near Secaucus Junction at Turnpike Exit 15X to increase capacity during the World Cup.
NJ Transit plans to contract with Transportation Management Services, Inc. and A Yankee Line, Inc. for the extra buses.

title: US to Boost Public Transit for the 2026 FIFA World Cup USDOT to Upgrade Transportation in Host Cities to Ensure Seamless Travel: Everything You Need to Know - Travel And Tour World
snippet: US to Boost Public Transit for the 2026 FIFA World Cup USDOT to Upgrade Transportation in Host Cities to Ensure Seamless Travel: Everything You Need to Know  Travel And Tour World
content:
Published on March 4, 2026
Ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, the U.S. Department of Transportation (USDOT) is set to significantly boost public transit systems across host cities, ensuring seamless travel for millions of fans. This major investment in transportation infrastructure will enhance connectivity and improve transit services, making it easier for visitors to navigate bustling cities and reach the stadiums on time. With the USDOT’s commitment to upgrading transportation in these key locations, the United States is preparing not just for the largest sporting event in history but for a lasting transformation in urban mobility. Here’s everything you need to know about this ambitious initiative and how it will impact fans and host cities alike.
U.S. Transportation Secretary, Sean P. Duffy, shared his commitment to ensuring a smooth travel experience for visitors attending the World Cup. The funding will support the expansion of public transit services, allowing host cities to meet the increased demand expected during match days and major public events. The new initiatives will ensure fans have reliable, efficient, and timely transportation to stadiums and other key locations.
“The DOT is determined to make the United States ready to host the most spectacular World Cup in history,” said Duffy. “Our collaboration with local transit agencies aims to create an exceptional experience for fans, ensuring they can get to the games without any hitches.”
The $100 million investment, which is part of the Consolidated Appropriations Act 2026, will be used by local transit agencies for various purposes. The funds will help improve planning, expand transit capacity, and cover operating expenses associated with the World Cup, such as additional buses, trains, and enhanced infrastructure. Host cities will be required to obligate these funds within one fiscal year following the conclusion of the event, ensuring that improvements are implemented quickly and efficiently.
Advertisement
Funding will be distributed based on a formula that accounts for factors such as the number of matches hosted and the stadium capacity in each location. This targeted allocation will allow cities with larger crowds to receive the necessary resources to handle the influx of people. The formula aims to ensure that each host city has the resources required to provide a seamless transportation experience to both visitors and residents.
This funding is not just about improving public transportation during the World Cup. According to Andrew Giuliani, Executive Director of the White House Task Force on the 2026 FIFA World Cup, this initiative will contribute to the long-term growth and development of U.S. cities. “These investments are about more than just moving fans they’re about preparing our communities for the largest sporting event in history,” Giuliani remarked. “By strengthening our public transit systems, we ensure that the world will see America at its best, not only during the World Cup but for many years to come.”
Advertisement
Advertisement
The broader impact of these improvements will extend beyond the event itself. Strengthened transit systems can continue to benefit host cities long after the final whistle of the World Cup. Enhanced transportation networks will provide long-term value to local residents, improve accessibility, and help mitigate congestion in high-traffic areas. For visitors, a smooth journey between their accommodations, event venues, and tourist attractions will make their trip to the U.S. even more enjoyable.
To make the most of this funding, the FTA has developed a toolkit and frequently asked questions (FAQ) resource for transit agencies. This comprehensive guide is designed to help local agencies navigate the grant process and optimize the use of the funds. By equipping transit agencies with the tools they need, the USDOT ensures that the investment delivers real and lasting improvements to the transportation infrastructure.

Advertisement
As the 2026 FIFA World Cup approaches, the United States is preparing not just for a global sporting spectacle but for a lasting transformation in its urban transportation landscape. The $100 million investment serves as a catalyst for a more connected, accessible, and vibrant future, paving the way for a better travel experience for both locals and visitors alike.
In conclusion, the U.S. Department of Transportation (USDOT) is taking decisive action to enhance public transit in the host cities for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. By investing in vital transportation upgrades, USDOT is ensuring seamless travel for the millions of fans expected to attend the event. These improvements will not only help manage the increased demand for transit services during match days but also lay the groundwork for long-term benefits for local communities. The planned upgrades will provide smoother, more efficient transportation to and from stadiums and key attractions, enhancing the overall experience for both visitors and residents. With this funding, the USDOT is playing a critical role in preparing the U.S. to host the most spectacular World Cup in history, while also fostering a more connected, accessible, and vibrant future for host cities.

title: North Texas transportation plans in works for 2026 FIFA World Cup - FOX 4 News Dallas-Fort Worth
snippet: North Texas transportation plans in works for 2026 FIFA World Cup  FOX 4 News Dallas-Fort Worth
content:
North Texas transportation plans in works for 2026 FIFA World Cup
ARLINGTON, Texas - There are just over eight months until the 2026 FIFA World Cup, with North Texas hosting more matches than any other location. The games will be played at AT&T Stadium, but there are venues spread across North Texas for other events.
Thursday, transportation planners talked about the logistics of how visitors will get from place to place during the world's biggest sporting event.
North Texas FIFA transportation planning
More than 100,000 daily visitors are expected to converge on the region. Arlington will play host to nine matches at the stadium, the most of any host city.
The City of Dallas will host the tournament’s international broadcast center. Fort Worth also has a major role in accommodating fans.
Therefore, mobility is a huge consideration with planning underway now for months.
The North Texas Central Council of Governments presented the latest overview of the plan at their Thursday meeting. Every major transportation entity is involved.
NCTCOG
The plans include enhanced versions of service from Trinity Rail Express, Dallas Area Rapid Transit, local bus systems, shuttle buses, private shuttles and ride-share companies.
Traffic flow will be critical, with the possibility of reversible managed lanes during peak travel times.
While it’s believed many local fans may drive to attend matches, a more concentrated planning effort is focused on moving visitors about on public transit.
What they're saying:
Natalie Bettger with NTCCOG spoke about their plan at the meeting.
"The good thing about our stadium in this area is we have a lot of parking. Unlike other host cities, they don't have the opportunity to have a lot of parking. The challenge we have is having transit to the stadium, and so we have offered transit to the stadium before we utilize the Trinity Railway Express and we charter folks in down from the Center Port Station. So we do plan to have transit. We expect a large number of fans to attend by transit, and that's what we're balancing with NTCCOG and the transit agencies to ensure that we can provide that service for the World Cup," Bettger said.
Transportation details coming in December
What's next:
This is the second draft of the current transportation plan. More of the details will unfold in December, when specific teams and matches will be revealed.
Times for the matches and specifics like which teams will stay where at the designated base camps.
Lots of moving parts are still coming together. Thursday even heard discussions of working with major freight trains to assure no railway crossings would be blocked by passing trains at peak times during the games.
Further plans include the use of technology like mobile phone apps with real-time updates and info about options for getting around.
The Source: Information in this article came from FOX 4 coverage at a public NTCCOG meeting.

title: TSA to Upgrade Security Screening Technology at FIFA World Cup 2026 Host Airports - Airport Industry-News
snippet: TSA to Upgrade Security Screening Technology at FIFA World Cup 2026 Host Airports  Airport Industry-News
content:
The Transportation Security Administration (TSA) has awarded a multi-million-dollar contract to Rohde & Schwarz for the supply of advanced passenger security screening equipment at US airports in host cities for the FIFA World Cup 2026.
Under the agreement, TSA will deploy Rohde & Schwarz QPS201 advanced imaging technology (AIT) security scanners at selected airport checkpoints. The scanners are intended to support passenger screening operations during the tournament, which will be hosted across multiple cities in the US, Canada and Mexico.
Rohde & Schwarz’s contract covers the delivery of QPS201 systems to airports serving host cities in the US. The award will also contribute to ongoing manufacturing and employment activity at its facilities in Maryland and Texas.
The QPS201 scanner received TSA qualification in 2022, authorising its use at US passenger security screening checkpoints. It is also certified to standards set by TSA and the European Civil Aviation Conference (ECAC). More than 100 QPS201 systems are currently in operation at US airports, with over 2,000 deployed globally.
Frank Dunn, CEO of Rohde & Schwarz USA, Inc. said:
We are thrilled to receive this award to deliver QPS201’s high-volume and passenger-friendly on-person security screening technology to modernise checkpoints at the airports of cities hosting the matches. TSA’s continued investment in the QPS will also further expand Rohde & Schwarz’s economic impact as we grow and create jobs at our facilities in Maryland and Texas.
The QPS201 uses millimetre wave radio frequency technology to detect concealed items on passengers. Scans are completed in milliseconds, and the system features an open design with a hands-down scanning position, intended to support accessibility and passenger throughput.
TSA’s investment forms part of broader preparations for major international events expected to increase passenger volumes at US airports over the coming years, including the FIFA World Cup 2026.
Andreas Haegele, Vice President of Microwave Imaging said:
We are proud TSA is investing in modernising security checkpoints at the Soccer World Cup 2026 host city airports with our high-performance QPS201 technology platform. Rohde & Schwarz is deeply committed to our partnership with TSA. We will continue to develop and deliver innovative and effective on-person screening solutions to make airport security more efficient and convenient in the upcoming mega decade of travel including the Soccer World Cup, America's 250th Anniversary and the Olympic Games.

title: US Prepares Airports With Biometric Tech for 2026 World Cup - Mexico Business News
snippet: US Prepares Airports With Biometric Tech for 2026 World Cup  Mexico Business News
content:
US Prepares Airports With Biometric Tech for 2026 World Cup
The United States has begun installing biometric electronic gates at major airports in preparation for an anticipated surge of international visitors during the 2026 FIFA World Cup. The rollout also aligns with upcoming large-scale events, including the nation’s 250th anniversary celebrations and the 2028 Olympic and Paralympic Games in Los Angeles.
The first eGates were installed on Aug. 26 at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, the country’s busiest international hub. The Transportation Security Administration (TSA) said the pilot program will expand to Ronald Reagan National Airport in Washington, DC, and Seattle-Tacoma International Airport before the end of the month. CLEAR, a private identity verification company, is partnering with TSA to implement the system.
The biometric eGates verify passengers’ identities by matching their facial image with identity documents and boarding passes. “Once identity and clearance are confirmed, passengers proceed directly to physical screening, bypassing the TSA podium while still undergoing all security screening,” CLEAR said.
The system is currently available as an opt-in feature for CLEAR Plus members. CLEAR stressed that its access to data is limited: “CLEAR transmits only limited data (live photo, boarding pass, ID photo used for enrollment, and identity information) — it has no access to watchlists, cannot override TSA gate decisions, and does not manually open gates.”
TSA highlighted the partnership as part of a broader effort to improve security screening through public-private collaboration. “TSA’s partnership with CLEAR on eGates is one example of how we are working with stakeholders both inside and outside the government to improve hospitality and security for the American traveler,” said TSA Acting Deputy Administrator Adam Stahl. TSA also noted that the pilot program is being implemented at no cost to taxpayers.
The introduction of biometric screening reflects a broader trend at TSA. The agency has been expanding facial recognition verification, with plans to deploy it at more than 400 airports in the coming years. Last month, TSA issued a request for information to explore additional “innovative technology” for improving passenger screening.
However, the increasing reliance on biometric tools has raised privacy concerns. Electronic gates collect sensitive personal information, including names, dates of birth, nationalities, passport numbers, photos, and biometric data such as facial scans or fingerprints. The involvement of private companies in processing this data has drawn scrutiny from lawmakers. In November, a bipartisan group in Congress urged the Department of Homeland Security’s inspector general to review TSA’s use of facial recognition. DHS Inspector General Joseph Cuffari confirmed earlier this year that his office would audit TSA’s deployment of the technology.

title: CLEAR to Launch eGate Pilot Program at Select U.S. Airports Ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026 - Yahoo Finance
snippet: CLEAR to Launch eGate Pilot Program at Select U.S. Airports Ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026  Yahoo Finance
content:
Public-private partnership aims to drive faster, more secure travel at host city airports across the country at no cost to taxpayers
NEW YORK, Aug. 19, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- CLEAR (NYSE: YOU), the secure identity company, today announced the launch of biometric eGates to help modernize America’s airports ahead of the FIFA World Cup 2026 games.
CLEAR is investing to improve security and the traveler experience at no cost to taxpayers. Automated eGates are designed to provide a scalable, secure solution to expedite the passenger experience and enhance security. The innovation builds on the Transportation Security Administration's (TSA) efforts to modernize and streamline U.S. airport security checkpoints, with a specific focus on enhancing security, hospitality, and the passenger experience. This first of its kind pilot represents an innovative public–private partnership that enables TSA to scale identity verification and streamline airport processing just in time for the World Cup. With more than 20 million international visitors expected to fly to the U.S. for the tournament — and domestic travel surging through U.S. host cities — checkpoint infrastructure will face unprecedented pressure. As part of this strategic partnership, CLEAR is investing to improve security and the traveler experience at no cost to taxpayers.
Starting in August 2025, these eGates will debut at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport (ATL), Ronald Reagan National Airport (DCA), and Seattle–Tacoma International Airport (SEA), with CLEAR planning to expand the eGate implementation across its nationwide network of airports in preparation for the World Cup, America’s 250th anniversary celebration, and continuing growth in domestic air travel.
“We are proud to help make America’s airports great ahead of the World Cup. The U.S. should be leading on security, hospitality, and the travel experience,” said CLEAR CEO Caryn Seidman Becker. “This partnership with TSA is a powerful example of how public and private sectors can work together to enhance security and improve the aviation ecosystem. CLEAR is investing in this technology at no cost to taxpayers to modernize our checkpoints and ensure America’s airports are ready to meet global expectations. CLEAR’s secure identity platform will help unlock a better, safer, and more frictionless experience for all travelers.”
CLEAR’s eGates are designed to conduct real-time biometric verification, matching a traveler’s facial image with their identity document and boarding pass. TSA retains complete operational control: triggering gate access, conducting security vetting, and enforcing government security requirements. CLEAR transmits only limited data (live photo, boarding pass, ID photo used for enrollment and identity information)--it has no access to watchlists, cannot override TSA gate decisions, and does not manually open gates. Once identity and clearance are confirmed, passengers proceed directly to physical screening, bypassing the TSA podium while still undergoing all security screening.

CLEAR's mission is to strengthen security and create frictionless experiences. With over 33 million Members and a growing network of partners across the world, CLEAR's secure identity platform is transforming the way people live, work, and travel. Whether you are traveling, at the stadium, or on your phone, CLEAR connects you to the things that make you, you—making everyday experiences easier, more secure, and friction-free. CLEAR is committed to privacy done right. Members are always in control of their own information, and we never sell Member data. For more information, visit clearme.com.

title: Mexico’s FIFA 2026 Strategy Hinges on Six Key Airports - Mexico Business News
snippet: Mexico’s FIFA 2026 Strategy Hinges on Six Key Airports  Mexico Business News
content:
Mexico’s FIFA 2026 Strategy Hinges on Six Key Airports
The countdown to the FIFA World Cup 2026 has officially begun—a historic tournament hosted for the first time across three countries. Mexico will stage 13 matches in Guadalajara, Monterrey, and Mexico City, expecting an influx of 9.7 million tourists and attendees. To prepare, the federal government has committed MX$225 billion (US$12 billion) to upgrade six key airports.
This investment aims to enhance connectivity and logistics for one of the world’s largest sporting events. Projects include improvements at Mexico City’s Felipe Ángeles International Airport (AIFA), the primary FIFA-designated hub, alongside capacity upgrades at Mexico City International Airport (AICM), Toluca, and Cuernavaca airports.
More than 5 million visitors are expected in Mexico City, where the iconic Estadio Azteca—now Estadio Banorte—will host five matches. AICM is undergoing major renovations, with MX$8 billion allocated for terminal expansions, runway upgrades, and modernized security systems. According to Juan José Padilla Olmos, head, Mexico City Airport Group, 19 construction projects and 32 aviation infrastructure upgrades are underway.
Toluca International Airport and Cuernavaca’s General Mariano Matamoros Airport are being reactivated to manage overflow traffic and reduce congestion at the capital’s main hubs. These airports will handle regional air traffic, offering improved operations and passenger services during the tournament.
Guadalajara, expecting 2.5 million visitors, will host four matches at Estadio Akron. Investments focus on urban infrastructure, including a MX$2.6 billion (US$137 million) overhaul of the Chapala highway, a key route to Guadalajara International Airport. Governor Pablo Lemus highlighted the addition of bike lanes, pedestrian walkways, and lighting systems. Innovation events and tech initiatives are also planned to boost local tourism.
Monterrey anticipates 2.2 million visitors for four matches at Estadio BBVA. The city is leveraging its modern Monterrey International Airport while prioritizing infrastructure projects like a second runway, highway expansions, metro line additions, and the “FIFA Corridor,” a pedestrian and cultural development. Water sustainability and hospitality upgrades are also underway.
Despite these efforts in host cities, national infrastructure spending has declined. Public investment fell 19% in the first four months of 2025—the steepest drop since 2019—following a surge last year to complete outgoing administration projects like the Mayan Train and Dos Bocas refinery. This decline, coupled with a slowing economy and logistical challenges, has raised concerns among experts.
“Infrastructure investment is a critical signal of economic health, legal certainty, and planning. Pausing that momentum sends the wrong message,” said Tamón Takahashi, Chief Economist, TKA Analytica.

title: Clear partners with TSA to roll out eGates at major airports ahead of FIFA 2026 - Yahoo Creators
snippet: Clear partners with TSA to roll out eGates at major airports ahead of FIFA 2026  Yahoo Creators
content:
Clear partners with TSA to roll out eGates at major airports ahead of FIFA 2026
The TSA-backed pilot program will test facial recognition eGates in Atlanta, D.C., & Seattle, aiming to reduce wait times before a record-breaking travel year.
As the U.S. prepares to welcome over 20 million international visitors for the FIFA World Cup 2026, CLEAR, the secure identity company, is rolling out a futuristic airport innovation aimed at transforming the way Americans and the world travel. The company announced today that it is launching a biometric eGate pilot program at select airports, part of a larger effort to modernize airport security while enhancing traveler convenience.
For millions of travelers expected to fly during the tournament, this could mean fewer lines, faster screening, and a much more seamless experience. And remarkably, all of this is happening at no cost to taxpayers.
Are biometric eGates the future of airport security?
Starting in August 2025, travelers flying through Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport (ATL), Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport (DCA), and Seattle–Tacoma International Airport (SEA) may notice a new high-tech addition at TSA checkpoints: automated eGates powered by CLEAR’s secure identity platform.
The eGates are designed to verify a traveler’s identity using facial biometrics, matching the passenger's live image to their government-issued ID and boarding pass. Once verified, travelers can proceed directly to physical screening, bypassing the traditional TSA podium, but not skipping security altogether.
This pilot program is a collaboration between CLEAR and the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), which retains full operational control. That means TSA still performs vetting, makes clearance decisions, and operates the security screening that follows. The gates simply automate and streamline the initial identity verification process.
“We are proud to help make America’s airports great ahead of the World Cup,” said CLEAR CEO Caryn Seidman Becker. “This partnership with TSA is a powerful example of how public and private sectors can work together to enhance security and improve the aviation ecosystem.”
Preparing for the World Cup and beyond
The FIFA World Cup 2026 will bring not only fans and athletes but also a massive spike in international and domestic air travel. With host cities spread across the U.S., the pressure on airport infrastructure is expected to be unlike anything experienced in decades.
CLEAR’s investment comes as part of a broader push to improve both airport security and traveler hospitality, especially in the face of large-scale global events. It also aligns with preparations for America’s 250th anniversary celebration in 2026 and a projected surge in domestic air travel over the coming years.
“CLEAR is investing in this technology at no cost to taxpayers to modernize our checkpoints and ensure America’s airports are ready to meet global expectations,” Becker emphasized.
How the CLEAR eGates work
So, what exactly will the experience look like for travelers?
Here’s a quick breakdown of how CLEAR’s eGates operate:
Step 1: The traveler approaches the CLEAR eGate and presents themselves for a live facial scan.
Step 2: The system matches the live image to the traveler’s pre-enrolled ID photo and boarding pass information.
Step 3: TSA, which monitors the eGate system, confirms security clearance and opens the gate.
Step 4: The traveler proceeds to the physical security screening as usual.
Importantly, CLEAR does not have access to government watchlists and cannot override TSA decisions. The company transmits only limited data: the traveler’s live photo, boarding pass, and enrollment-based ID information. TSA maintains full control over who gets through.
A step toward the “frictionless” airport
With over 33 million CLEAR members and partnerships across travel, sports, and digital identity platforms, the company is increasingly positioning itself as a key player in the future of movement, whether you're catching a flight or heading to a stadium.
This latest rollout of eGates could be the start of a much broader trend. If successful, CLEAR plans to expand the technology nationwide, particularly at key travel hubs, before the 2026 World Cup begins in June.
In an era when travelers are more tech-savvy and more impatient than ever, the demand for efficient, contactless, and secure identity verification is growing fast. CLEAR’s eGates aim to meet that demand head-on.
What travelers should know
If you're traveling through one of the pilot airports this fall, here’s what you should keep in mind:
You don’t need to be a CLEAR member to use the eGates, though membership may provide other benefits.
You’ll still go through TSA’s physical security screening.
The system uses facial recognition, so you may need to remove hats, sunglasses, and masks briefly for a match.
TSA officers will remain on-site and in control at all times.
Innovations like CLEAR’s biometric eGates could become the new norm for travelers, but only time will tell if the tech truly expedites the process.

title: TSA to Roll Out Touchless-ID Facial Recognition at 65 U.S. Airports Ahead of 2026 World Cup - VisaHQ
snippet: TSA to Roll Out Touchless-ID Facial Recognition at 65 U.S. Airports Ahead of 2026 World Cup  VisaHQ
content:
The Transportation Security Administration confirmed on Monday that its TSA PreCheck Touchless ID program—an opt-in system that uses facial recognition instead of physical IDs—is moving from pilot phase to nationwide deployment. By late spring, 65 airports will feature the biometric lanes, with priority given to cities hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, when international arrivals are expected to spike.
Under the system, enrolled PreCheck travelers upload a passport photo through their airline’s website or app. At the checkpoint, a camera verifies the live image against government databases; once matched, the traveler proceeds without presenting a passport or driver’s license. TSA says the process cuts verification time to about five seconds and reduces document-handling, a lingering public-health concern since the COVID-19 pandemic.
International visitors heading to the U.S. for marquee events like the World Cup will still need to secure the appropriate travel authorization before they ever reach a biometric checkpoint. VisaHQ’s online platform (https://www.visahq.com/united-states/) guides applicants through ESTA requests and full visa packages, keeping pace with evolving security measures such as Touchless ID. By streamlining paperwork and alerting users to real-time requirement changes, VisaHQ helps both individual travelers and corporate travel departments avoid last-minute surprises.
Privacy advocates and several senators immediately raised red flags. A bipartisan “Traveler Privacy Protection Act” introduced last year would bar TSA from making facial scans effectively compulsory and would require prompt deletion of biometric data. TSA counters that photos are encrypted, stored for less than 24 hours, and never shared with law-enforcement databases—assurances critics call insufficient after a 2024 subcontractor breach exposed 190,000 images.
Airlines are enthusiastic. Delta, United, Alaska, Southwest and JetBlue are already integrated, and industry group Airlines for America predicts 40 percent of domestic PreCheck users will opt in during the first year. Corporate-travel managers see the technology as a boon for tight connections and duty-of-care tracking, though they caution companies to brief employees on opt-out rights for jurisdictions like Illinois that restrict facial recognition.
For now the program remains voluntary, but experts say widespread adoption could pressure holdouts to participate—much like the gradual normalization of PreCheck itself. Travelers concerned about data use can decline the scan and undergo a traditional ID check, though TSA officers warn that separate queues may lengthen wait times during the World Cup rush.

title: DiDi Pushes to Operate at Mexican Airports for 2026 World Cup - Mexico Business News
snippet: DiDi Pushes to Operate at Mexican Airports for 2026 World Cup  Mexico Business News
content:
DiDi Pushes to Operate at Mexican Airports for 2026 World Cup
DiDi is urging the Mexican federal government and airport authorities to establish a regulatory framework allowing its vehicles to operate within airport terminals in cities hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup. The move seeks to meet the expected surge in transportation demand during the tournament, which will take place across Mexico, the United States, and Canada.
“The World Cup is a great opportunity to invite the government to create modern regulations that transparently allow platforms to operate freely at airports,” said Juan Panamá, CEO, DiDi Latin America. He emphasized that mobility apps will play a major role during the event.
Currently, airport access in Mexico is largely limited to licensed taxi services, creating tension between traditional operators and app-based mobility platforms. DiDi argues that airport inclusion would increase competition and reduce passenger wait times.
The company sees the World Cup as an opportunity to improve traveler experience and generate income for thousands of drivers and delivery partners nationwide. “We are working to create an accessible platform and ensure drivers and partners benefit from this tourism opportunity,” Panamá said.
In host states—Mexico City, Jalisco, and Nuevo Leon—DiDi has reported measurable economic contributions. Siegfried Eisenmenger, DiDi’s Latin America Public Policy Director, highlighted the company’s impact on state GDP: 0.48% in Jalisco, 0.64% in Nuevo León, and 0.75% in Mexico City.
Overall, DiDi estimates its total contribution to Mexico’s economy at MX$118 billion, or 0.47% of national GDP. The company also reports a social impact value of roughly MX$216 billion (0.86% of GDP), equivalent to providing food baskets for 91 million people.

title: AICM ranks 11th among the 16 airports for the 2026 World Cup - MEXICONOW
snippet: AICM ranks 11th among the 16 airports for the 2026 World Cup  MEXICONOW
content:
AICM ranks 11th among the 16 airports for the 2026 World Cup
With 11 months to go before the start of the 2026 World Cup, which will be held in Mexico, the United States, and Canada, Mexican airports rank among those with the lowest volume of operations and passengers compared to those in other host cities.
Mexico City International Airport (AICM) ranked 11th out of 16 with 322,427 operations in 2024. Guadalajara ranked 13th with 154,862 flights, and Monterrey closed the list with 112,584 operations, according to data from Cirium and the Airports Council International (ACI).
In contrast, Atlanta Airport topped the ranking with 766,787 operations, followed by Dallas/Fort Worth (743,203) and Los Angeles (514,031). Dallas will host nine matches, including a semifinal, and will be home to FIFA's communications center.
In terms of passenger numbers, Mexican airports also appear at the bottom of the list. The AICM ranked tenth with 45.3 million passengers, Guadalajara 14th with 17.8 million, and Monterrey 15th with 13.5 million, just above Kansas City (12.1 million).
At the top of the list are Atlanta (108 million), Dallas (87.8), and Los Angeles (76.5). Of the Canadian venues, Toronto Pearson transported 46.8 million (9th place) and Vancouver 26.2 million (13th).
Ahead of the tournament, Mexican airports are undergoing modernization. In the case of the AICM, its remodeling will be supported by the AIFA and the airports of Toluca and Cuernavaca, to serve visitors who will attend the Azteca Stadium, where five matches will be played, including the World Cup opening on June 11. Guadalajara and Monterrey will also upgrade their facilities as part of the national plan.
